paper_metadata.title: Do Thinking Tokens Help with Safety?

research_equation.problem: Current reasoning models use thinking tokens to improve benchmark performance, but it is unknown whether this 'thinking' genuinely improves safety deliberation or merely rationalizes pre-determined outcomes.
research_equation.gap: The assumption that thinking tokens enable safety deliberation remains unvalidated. No systematic study has examined whether safety decisions are actually made during the thinking process or are predetermined.
research_equation.approach: Train linear probes on first-token hidden representations → Measure Fisher discriminant across thinking traces → Analyze continuation variance given fixed prefixes → Annotate thinking trace stance → Evaluate whether defenses induce deliberation
research_equation.solution: Safety outcomes are strongly predictable from the first token before thinking begins. Thinking is prefix completion, not genuine deliberation. Current safety defenses fail to change this.
research_equation.evaluation: AUROC, balanced accuracy, Fisher discriminant ratio, majority-label flip rate, oscillation statistics, ASR-ORR Pareto analysis
research_equation.limitation: Open-weight models only; visible thinking tokens only; safety/refusal behavior only; linear probes only; frontier LLM-based annotation

pass1_problem_engagement.question: Do thinking tokens actually help with safety in reasoning models?
pass1_problem_engagement.intuition: The community believes that 'thinking before answering' should improve safety by giving models space to consider safety principles.
pass1_problem_engagement.what_they_did: Trained logistic probes on first-token hidden representations across 4 model families to see if safety outcomes are already determined before thinking.
pass1_problem_engagement.key_numbers: AUROC 0.84-0.95, BAcc ~88%, 91-97% of decisions unchanged given fixed prefix
pass1_problem_engagement.core_claim: Safety behavior is far less deliberative than commonly assumed — outcomes are already locked by the first visible token.
pass1_problem_engagement.surprising_element: Even text-level deliberation signals (~74%) occur when the response distribution is already locked.

experiment1.name: First-token probe experiment
experiment1.setup: Train logistic regression probe on last-layer hidden representation of first visible token across 4 model families, 5-fold stratified cross-validation, Youden's J cutoff
experiment1.what_it_means: Safety outcome is almost fully determined before the model generates any visible thinking tokens.

experiment2.name: Continuation variance analysis
experiment2.setup: Fix prefix of thinking trace at different positions, sample multiple continuations with different random seeds
experiment2.result: 70-97% of decisions unchanged; most changes are 'flipped to worse'
experiment2.what_it_means: The thinking trace functions like prefix completion — early tokens constrain subsequent generation strongly.

experiment3.name: Thinking trace stance annotation
experiment3.setup: Three frontier LLM auditors (GPT-5.4, Gemini 3 Pro, Sonnet 4.6) annotate sentence-level stance. Fleiss κ=0.67 agreement.
experiment3.result: ~74% of deliberation segments occur when the response distribution is already locked
experiment3.what_it_means: Surface-level deliberation is mostly post-hoc rationalization.

experiment4.name: Defense evaluation
experiment4.setup: 9 safety defenses (3 inference-time, 6 training-based) × 4 base models, ASR/ORR on multiple benchmarks
experiment4.result: No defense improves both ASR and ORR. All move along the tradeoff curve.
experiment4.what_it_means: Current defenses do not induce genuine deliberation; they shift the refusal threshold.

experiment5.name: Fisher discriminant analysis
experiment5.setup: Compute Fisher discriminant ratio between refusal and compliance representations across normalized thinking positions
experiment5.result: Valley signature: high → low → high separability across all 9 models tested
experiment5.what_it_means: Early decision concentration is a universal phenomenon across model families.

experiment6.name: Thinking budget experiments
experiment6.setup: Truncate/extend thinking to different fractions of natural length; evaluate ASR/ORR
experiment6.result: More thinking → more compliance; extended thinking past natural stopping → no additional change
experiment6.what_it_means: More thinking does not improve safety; it tends to increase compliance.

pass3_synthesis_and_extension.what_we_learned: Thinking tokens do NOT help with safety in the way the community assumed. The safety decision is made before thinking begins, and subsequent thinking tokens are largely prefix completion that rationalizes the pre-determined outcome. This is robustly observed across 9 models from 5+ families and 2 parameter scales.
pass3_synthesis_and_extension.why_it_matters: Current safety alignment approaches that rely on 'thinking' or 'deliberation' during inference may be fundamentally misguided. The research community needs new methods to induce genuine safety deliberation.
open_questions: Can training methods be designed to make safety decisions truly deliberative rather than pre-determined?
What mechanism causes the first token to encode the safety outcome so strongly?
Do thinking tokens in non-safety domains (math, code) also function as prefix completion?
How does this interact with hidden reasoning channels (vs visible thinking)?
Can first-token probes be used as a safety early-warning system at inference time?
pass3_synthesis_and_extension.broader_impact: This work challenges a core assumption underlying safety research on reasoning models. It suggests that the 'thinking' in current reasoning models is shallower than believed, and that genuine AI safety deliberation remains an open problem.

challenge_module_map.challenge: Safety decisions are pre-determined before thinking begins
potential_approaches: Train models to defer safety decisions until after thorough thinking
Develop training objectives that penalize early lock-in of safety outcomes
Design architectures where safety evaluation happens in a separate, late-attending module

challenge_module_map.challenge: Current safety defenses fail to induce genuine deliberation
potential_approaches: Design defenses that target first-token hidden representations rather than surface-level thinking
Develop adversarial training that rewards late-stage safety deliberation
Create probing-based feedback loops during training to encourage deliberative thinking

challenge_module_map.challenge: Thinking traces are predominantly post-hoc rationalization
potential_approaches: Use probing during training to reduce the gap between early encoding and later thinking
Design models where the thinking process causally influences the final decision

challenge_module_map.challenge: More thinking tends to increase compliance (both harmful and benign)
potential_approaches: Develop thinking length regularization that penalizes compliance without genuine deliberation
Design calibrated thinking budgets per request type

challenge_module_map.challenge: Inter-annotator agreement on thinking stance is good but not perfect
potential_approaches: Develop automated stance classifiers with higher reliability
Use ground-truth from controllable experiments rather than LLM judgments

methodological_contribution: First-token probing as diagnostic for deliberation depth
Fisher discriminant trajectory analysis for safety reasoning dynamics
Continuation variance as measure of thinking's causal influence
Systematic multi-defense, multi-model ASR-ORR Pareto analysis
